---
title: "Visualizations and inference for 2D categorical data"
subtitle: "<br>36-315: Statistical Graphics and Visualization, Summer 2026"
footer:  "[36-315-summer26.netlify.app](https://36-315-summer26.netlify.app)"
format:
  revealjs:
    embed-resources: true
    height: 900
    width: 1600
    theme: theme.scss
    smaller: true
    slide-number: c/t
    linestretch: 1.4
    code-line-numbers: false
    html-math-method:
      method: mathjax
      url: "https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/mathjax@3/es5/tex-mml-chtml.js"
---

```{r}
#| include: false
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  echo = TRUE,
  message = FALSE,
  warning = FALSE,
  fig.align = "center"
)
ggplot2::theme_set(ggplot2::theme_gray(base_size = 20))
```

## Summarizing 2D categorical data

```{r}
library(tidyverse)
titanic <- read_csv("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/qntkhvn/36-315-summer26/refs/heads/master/data/titanic.csv") |> 
  filter(!is.na(Survived) & !is.na(Pclass)) |> 
  mutate(Survived = factor(Survived),
         Pclass = factor(Pclass))
```

Using the titanic data, let's look at:

* `Survived`: survival outcome (0/1)

```{r}
table(titanic$Survived)
```

* `Class`: cabin class (1st/2nd/3rd)

```{r}
table(titanic$Pclass)
```

## Summarizing 2D categorical data

Two-way table (or contingency table, cross tabulation, crosstab)

<br>

```{r}
table("Survived" = titanic$Survived, "Pclass" = titanic$Pclass)
```

<br>

```{r}
xtabs(~ Survived + Pclass, data = titanic)
```


## Joint, marginal, and conditional distributions

```{r}
xtabs(~ Survived + Pclass, data = titanic) |> 
  addmargins()
```

* Column and row sums: marginal distributions

* Values within rows: conditional distribution for `Pclass` given `Survived`

* Values within columns: conditional distribution for `Survived` given `Pclass`

* Values within cells: joint distribution for `Survived` and `Pclass`

* Bottom right: total number of observations

## Joint, marginal, and conditional probabilities

::: columns
::: {.column}

* Joint distribution: the intersection

  * e.g., $P(\texttt{Survived} = 1, \texttt{Pclass} = 3)$

* Marginal distribution: row sums or column sums

  * e.g., $P(\texttt{Survived} = 1)$, $P(\texttt{Pclass} = 3)$

* Conditional distribution:<br>probability of $\texttt{Survived}$ given $\texttt{Pclass}$

  * e.g., $P(\texttt{Survived} = 1 \mid \texttt{Pclass} = 3)$

    $\displaystyle \qquad \quad = \frac{P(\texttt{Survived} = 1, \texttt{Pclass} = 3)}{P(\texttt{Pclass} = 3)}$
:::

::: {.column}

<br>

```{r}
table(titanic$Survived, titanic$Pclass)
```

<br>

```{r}
prop.table(table(titanic$Survived, titanic$Pclass))
```

:::
:::


## Connecting distributions to visualizations

* For two categorical variables $A$ and $B$

  * Marginal distributions: $P(A)$ and $P(B)$

  * Conditional distributions: $P(A \mid B)$ and $P(B \mid A)$

  * Joint distribution: $P(A, B)$
  
. . .

* We use bar charts to visualize marginal distributions for categorical variables...

. . . 

* And we will use more bar charts (**stacked** and **side-by-side**) to visualize conditional and joint distributions

## Stacked bar charts

::: columns
::: {.column}
* Stacked bar chart: a bar chart of spine charts

* Easy to see marginal of `Survived`

  * i.e., $P($`x`$)$

* Can see conditional of `Pclass` | `Survived` 

  * i.e., $P($`fill` $\mid$ `x`$)$

* Harder to see conditional of `Survived` | `Pclass`

  * i.e., $P($`x` $\mid$ `fill`$)$

```{r}
#| eval: false
titanic |> 
  ggplot(aes(x = Survived, fill = Pclass)) +
  geom_bar() 
```
:::

::: {.column}
```{r}
#| echo: false
#| fig-height: 9
titanic |> 
  ggplot(aes(x = Survived, fill = Pclass)) +
  geom_bar() 
```
:::
:::


## Side-by-side bar charts

::: columns
::: {.column}
* Side-by-side (grouped/dodged) bar chart: a bar chart of bar charts

* Easy to see conditional of `Pclass` | `Survived` 

  * i.e., $P($`fill` $\mid$ `x`$)$

* Can see conditional of `Survived` | `Pclass` 

  * i.e., $P($`x` $\mid$ `fill`$)$

* Harder to see marginals...

```{r}
#| eval: false
titanic |> 
  ggplot(aes(x = Survived, fill = Pclass)) +
  geom_bar(position = "dodge")
```
:::

::: {.column}
```{r}
#| echo: false
#| fig-height: 9
titanic |> 
  ggplot(aes(x = Survived, fill = Pclass)) +
  geom_bar(position = "dodge")
```
:::
:::

## Which one do you prefer?

::: columns
::: {.column}
```{r}
#| echo: false
#| fig-height: 9
titanic |> 
  ggplot(aes(x = Survived, fill = Pclass)) +
  geom_bar() 
```
:::

::: {.column}
```{r}
#| echo: false
#| fig-height: 9
titanic |> 
  ggplot(aes(x = Survived, fill = Pclass)) +
  geom_bar(position = "dodge") 
```
:::
:::

## Categorical heatmaps

::: columns
::: {.column}

* Use `geom_tile` to display joint distribution of two categorical variables

* Annotate tiles with labels of percentages using `geom_text()`

```{r}
#| eval: false
titanic |>
  group_by(Survived, Pclass) |>
  summarize(n = n(), 
            joint = n() / nrow(titanic),
            txt = paste(round(100 * joint, 2), "%")) |> 
  ggplot(aes(x = Survived, y = Pclass)) +
  geom_tile(aes(fill = n), color = "white") +
  geom_text(aes(label = txt)) +
  scale_fill_gradient2()
```
:::

::: {.column}
```{r}
#| echo: false
#| fig-height: 11
titanic |>
  group_by(Survived, Pclass) |>
  summarize(n = n(), 
            joint = n() / nrow(titanic),
            txt = paste(round(100 * joint, 2), "%")) |> 
  ggplot(aes(x = Survived, y = Pclass)) +
  geom_tile(aes(fill = n), color = "white") +
  geom_text(aes(label = txt)) +
  scale_fill_gradient2()
```
:::
:::

## Inference for 2D categorical data: Chi-squared test

::: columns
::: {.column}
* **Null hypothesis** ($H_0$) : variables $A$ and $B$ are independent of each other

  * e.g., no association between<br>`Survived` and `Pclass`

* Test statistic: $\displaystyle \chi^2 = \sum_i^{k_1} \sum_j^{k_2} \frac{(O_{ij} - E_{ij})^2}{E_{ij}}$

  * $O_{ij}$: observed counts in contingency table

  * $E_{ij}$: expected counts under $H_0$

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{ij} &= n \cdot P(A = a_i, B = b_j) \\
&= n \cdot P(A = a_i) P(B = b_j) \\
&= n \cdot \left( \frac{n_{i \cdot}}{n} \right) \left( \frac{ n_{\cdot j}}{n} \right)
\end{aligned}
$$
:::

::: {.column}

<br>
<br>

```{r}
chisq.test(table(titanic$Survived, titanic$Pclass))
```
:::
:::

## Visualizing independence with mosaic plots

Two variables are **independent** if knowing the level of one tells us nothing about the other<br>i.e., $P(A \mid B) = P(A)$, and $P(A, B) = P(A) \times P(B)$

::: columns
::: {.column}

* Mosaic plots: a spine chart of spine charts

* width: marginal distribution of `Survived` (columns)

* height: conditional of `Pclass` | `Survived`<br>(rows | columns)

* area: joint distribution

**Use a mosaic plot to visually check for independence**: whether all proportions are the same<br>(the boxes line up in a grid)

:::

::: {.column}

```{r}
#| eval: false
mosaicplot(table(titanic$Survived, titanic$Pclass),
           main = "Relationship between survival and cabin class")
```


```{r}
#| echo: false
#| fig-height: 8
par(cex.main = 2)
mosaicplot(table(titanic$Survived, titanic$Pclass), cex = 2,
           main = "Relationship between survival and cabin class")
```

:::
:::

## Residuals

Recall the test statistic: $\displaystyle \chi^2 = \sum_i^{k_1} \sum_j^{k_2} \frac{(O_{ij} - E_{ij})^2}{E_{ij}}$

Define the Pearson residuals: $\displaystyle r_{ij} = \frac{O_{ij} - E_{ij}}{\sqrt{E_{ij}}}$

Some rules of thumb:

* $r_{ij} \approx 0$: observed counts are close to expected counts

* $|r_{ij}| > 2$: significant at $\alpha = 0.05$

* very positive $r_{ij}$: higher than expected

* very negative $r_{ij}$: lower than expected

:::aside
In general, Pearson residuals are computed as $\displaystyle \frac{\text{residuals}}{\sqrt{\text{variance}}}$
:::

## Mosaic plots with residuals

* Boxes are shaded by Pearson residuals (`shade = TRUE`), reveal which combinations of<br>2 categorical variables (cells) are higher/lower than expected

* From Chi-squared test earlier: `Survival` and `Pclass` are associated. But how?

```{r}
#| eval: false
mosaicplot(table(titanic$Survived, titanic$Pclass), shade = TRUE, main = "Relationship between survival and cabin class")
```

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| fig-width: 18
#| fig-height: 9
par(cex.main = 2)
mosaicplot(table(titanic$Survived, titanic$Pclass), cex = 2,
           shade = TRUE, main = "Relationship between survival and cabin class")
```

## Mosaic plots with residuals

```{r}
#| eval: false
mosaicplot(table(titanic$Sex, titanic$Embarked), shade = TRUE, main = "Passenger sex appears independent of embarkation point")
```

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| fig-width: 18
#| fig-height: 9
par(cex.main = 2)
mosaicplot(table(titanic$Sex, titanic$Embarked), cex = 2,
           shade = TRUE, main = "Passenger sex appears independent of embarkation point")
```

## Beyond 2D: faceting

```{r}
#| fig-width: 16
#| fig-height: 8
titanic |> 
  ggplot(aes(x = Survived, fill = Pclass)) + 
  geom_bar(position = "dodge") +
  facet_wrap(~ Sex)
```

## Mosaic plots in the wild

1000 songs to hear before you die (Source: [The Guardian](https://www.theguardian.com/news/datablog/2009/mar/20/1))

<center>
![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/8/84/Mosaic-big.png?20090726044211){height=700}
</center>

## Replication

```{r}
#| eval: false
songs <- read_csv("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/ryurko/Spring23-36315-Data/master/1000songs.csv")

songs <- songs |> 
  mutate(
    decade = case_when(
      YEAR <= 1959 ~ "1910s-50s",
      YEAR %in% 1960:1969 ~ "1960s",
      YEAR %in% 1970:1979 ~ "1970s",
      YEAR %in% 1980:1989 ~ "1980s",
      YEAR %in% 1990:1999 ~ "1990s",
      YEAR >= 2000 ~ "2000s"
    )
   )

mosaicplot(table(songs$decade, songs$THEME), shade = TRUE, las = 1, main = "")
```

## Replication

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| fig-height: 9
#| fig-width: 15
songs <- read_csv("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/ryurko/Spring23-36315-Data/master/1000songs.csv")

songs <- songs |> 
  mutate(
    decade = case_when(
      YEAR <= 1959 ~ "1910s-50s",
      YEAR %in% 1960:1969 ~ "1960s",
      YEAR %in% 1970:1979 ~ "1970s",
      YEAR %in% 1980:1989 ~ "1980s",
      YEAR %in% 1990:1999 ~ "1990s",
      YEAR >= 2000 ~ "2000s"
    )
   )

mosaicplot(table(songs$decade, songs$THEME), shade = TRUE, las = 1, main = "", cex = 1.5)
```
